title: Cats vs. Dogs
text:
Figure 1: Votes and shares for Cats vs Dogs.





Dogs share: 66.7%, Cats share: 33.3%. Vote margin (Dogs − Cats): 14 votes = 33.3%.



Testing Cats proportion p against 0.5 using an exact binomial test.



    Exact binomial test

data:  14 and N
number of successes = 14, number of trials = 42, p-value = 0.04356
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.1956680 0.4954875
sample estimates:
probability of success 
             0.3333333 


Two‑sided p‑value: 0.0436 95% exact CI for p(Cats): 0.196 to 0.495
Express the Dogs lead as a margin in share terms, margin = p(Dogs) − p(Cats) = 1 − 2p(Cats). The corresponding 95% CI:


[1] 0.608663904 0.009025071
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95


Even the lower bound of the margin is positive, indicating a clear preference for Dogs.
How many flips would change the winner?
To tie, you’d need 7 votes to flip (7 Dogs → Cats). To give Cats a lead, you’d need 8 flips (8 votes).



Under p = 0.5 with N = 42, the probability to be at least this far from 50/50 (two‑sided) is the binomial test p‑value.
We can also simulate the Cats counts under 50/50:








Figure 2: Simulated Cats counts under a 50/50 world (10,000 polls, N = 42). The observed 14 is marked.





Fraction of simulations at least as extreme (two‑sided):


[1] 0.0467





Binomial likelihood for p(Cats) given 14 out of 42 votes:








Figure 3: Binomial likelihood L(p) for p(Cats) given 14/42 (scaled to max = 1).





With a flat prior Beta(1,1), the posterior is Beta(15,29). Credible interval and the probability Cats < 0.5 (i.e., Dogs lead):








Figure 4: Posterior for p(Cats) with Beta(1,1) prior (posterior = Beta(15,29)).





95% credible interval for p(Cats): [0.210, 0.485]
Posterior probability Dogs lead (p(Cats) < 0.5): 0.9842
Posterior predictive for a repeat poll of size 42: chance Cats win, tie, or Dogs win.


pp_cat_win     pp_tie pp_dog_win 
   0.05205    0.02760    0.92035 


Bayes factor for “not 50/50” vs “p = 0.5” using Jeffreys prior Beta(0.5,0.5).


[1] 1.319843


BF10 > 1 supports “not 50/50”; values around 3–10 are often called “moderate” evidence.





risk_difference      risk_ratio       odds_dogs        cohens_h 
      0.3333333       2.0000000       2.0000000       0.6796738 


Dogs lead by 33.3% in share. Dogs have about 2:1 the share of Cats and odds about 2:1. Cohen’s h ≈ 0.68 (medium‑to‑large difference; 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large).











Figure 5: Waffle chart: 42 voters, Cats = 14, Dogs = 28.

title: Hello there!
text: This site hosts the results from and ruminations on The Hallway Poll. The Hallway Poll was developed to help bring joy and intrigue to the WJCS 4232 suite and all those who pass through it. Please explore the site by using the navigation bar to explore.

title: Cheesy Snacks
text:
# Packages
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(broom)

set.seed(2026)

# Data
results <- tibble(
  option = c("Cheez-Its", "Goldfish"),
  votes  = c(21, 21)
) |>
  mutate(share = votes / sum(votes))

N <- sum(results$votes)

results

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  option    votes share
  <chr>     <dbl> <dbl>
1 Cheez-Its    21   0.5
2 Goldfish     21   0.5

title: Holiday Drinks
text:
I, myself, really love eggnog (you should really try Alton Browns Aged Eggnog Recipe)



“Other” is the single most popular choice, with 19 out of 38 votes (50%)
All named drinks (non-“Other”) combined (Eggnog + Mulled Wine + Seasonal Beer) also add up to 19, so its a perfect split: Named = 19, Other = 19
Among the named drinks, Mulled Wine leads (10), followed by Eggnog (6), then Seasonal Beer (3)

This isnt a scientific poll, just a fun whiteboard vote, so think of these numbers as a snapshot of who happened to drop by the office this week. I’m going to hope that the other eggnog lovers at EPA were enjoying it on a holiday vacation somewhere.











Figure 1: Votes and shares for each holiday drink.





Now lets split the drinks into “named drinks” and “other”:





group
votes
share




Named (Eggnog + Mulled Wine + Seasonal Beer)
19
0.5


Other
19
0.5





Named drinks total: 19 (50.0%)
Other: 19 (50.0%)



If people had no real preference and were equally likely to pick any option, would we often see a split as uneven as this?

If all four options were equally popular, we’d expect about 9–10 votes per option (38 total / 4 options).
Here we saw 19 for “Other” and just 3 for “Seasonal Beer,” which is quite uneven.

We can run a simple check (called a chi‑square test) that asks: “Are these numbers more uneven than we’d expect from random chance under a 25%‑each world?” It returns a p‑value. Think of it as “how surprising is this, if there were truly no differences?”



    Chi-squared test for given probabilities

data:  results$votes
X-squared = 15.263, df = 3, p-value = 0.001605


A small p‑value means “this pattern is hard to explain by chance if all options were equally liked.” If p is below about 0.05, most folks would say “there’s a real difference here.” In this poll, the p‑value is 0.001605, which suggests the preferences are not equal across the four options.
Which options were most “surprising” relative to equal splits? Compare what we saw to what we’d expect (9.5 per option):


# A tibble: 4 × 4
  drink         observed expected standardized_difference
  <chr>            <dbl>    <dbl>                   <dbl>
1 Other               19      9.5                    3.08
2 Mulled Wine         10      9.5                    0.16
3 Eggnog               6      9.5                   -1.14
4 Seasonal Beer        3      9.5                   -2.11



Positive numbers = above the equal‑share expectation (e.g., “Other”).
Negative numbers = below the equal‑share expectation (e.g., “Seasonal Beer”).




If we repeated this whiteboard poll with a similar crowd, the exact numbers would bounce around. The dogs below show the observed share (%) for each drink. The horizontal lines show the 95% confidence interval (aka plausible ranges).








Figure 2: Share estimates with 95% confidence intervals for each category.





“Other” sits well above 25% and its plausible range stays high.
Seasonal Beer sits well below 25%.
Mulled Wine and Eggnog are in the middle, with Mulled Wine ahead of Eggnog in this sample.



Two quick, approachable views:

If all options were truly equally popular, how often would “Other” land at 19 again? The chart below simulates many “equal‑preference” polls (everyone picks randomly among 4 options) and shows where “Other” would usually fall. The dashed line shows our observed 19.









Figure 3: If all four options were equally popular (25% each), ‘Other’ would usually land near 9–10—not 19.






If people’s true preferences looked like this week’s percentages, how often would “Other” be the top choice again? Below we simulate many re‑runs using this week’s percentages as a rough guide.









Figure 4: Simulating repeat polls using this week’s percentages. How often does each drink come out on top?





In words, with this week’s pattern, “Other” would win most of the time in a same‑size rerun. Mulled Wine would sometimes take the top spot, Eggnog and Seasonal Beer would rarely do so at this size.
A handy “how many votes would need to change?” rule of thumb: to overtake the leader “Other” (19) with second‑place Mulled Wine (10), you’d need about 5 people to switch from “Other” to “Mulled Wine.” That would make “Other” 14 and “Mulled Wine” 15, flipping the lead.











Figure 5: Each square is one vote. Colors show how the 38 votes are distributed.

title: Cheesy Snacks
text:
Counts and shares of votes








Figure 1: Votes and shares for each snack.

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Inference: does anyone lead in the poll?
text:
Inference: does anyone lead in the poll?
The natural null here is p = 0.5 for Cheez-Its, with N = 42



    Exact binomial test

data:  21 and 42
number of successes = 21, number of trials = 42, p-value = 1
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.3419476 0.6580524
sample estimates:
probability of success 
                   0.5 


The p-value is 1, a perfect tie gives no evidence either way. The 95% exact CI for the Cheez-Its share p is:


[1] 0.3419476 0.6580524
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95


We can also express uncertainty for the vote-margin (Cheez-Its minus Goldfish share). Share margin = 2p - 1, the 95% CI for the margin is just 2 x CI(p) - 1:


[1] -0.3161048  0.3161048
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Probability of a tie if it really is a fair 50/50 world of Cheez-Its and Goldfish
text:
Probability of a tie if it really is a fair 50/50 world of Cheez-Its and Goldfish
If each of the 42 voters is a fair coin flip, the probability of a tie is:


[1] 0.1223857


That’s about r percent(p_tie, accuracy = 0.1). Ties are not rare with samples this small.

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: What margins should we expect with N = 42 at p = 0.5
text:
What margins should we expect with N = 42 at p = 0.5
Simulate many polls of the same size from a fair process and look at the margin in votes.








Figure 2: Simulated vote margins if the true split is 50/50 (10,000 polls of size 42).





What fraction of simulated polls tie?


[1] 0.1241

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Likelihood and Bayesian view for the Cheez-Its share
text:
Likelihood and Bayesian view for the Cheez-Its share
The binomial likelihood for p given 21 successes in 42 trials:








Figure 3: Binomial likelihood L(p) for Cheez-Its share given 21/42.





If you use a flat prior Beta(1,1), the posterior is Beta(22,22). The 95% credible interval and posterior density:








Figure 4: Posterior density for p with a Beta(1,1) prior (posterior = Beta(22,22)).





The posterior probability that Cheez-Its leads (p > 0.5) under this symmetric prior is exactly 0.5.


[1] 0.5

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Effect size: Cohen’s h for proportions
text:
Effect size: Cohen’s h for proportions
Cohen’s h = 2asin(sqrt(p1)) − 2asin(sqrt(p2)). With p1 = p2 = 0.5, h = 0 (no effect).


numeric(0)

title: Cheesy Snacks
section: Lets try a waffle chart (one square per vote)
text:

text: Figure 5: Each square is one vote. Colors show how the 38 votes are distributed.

title: Toilet Paper Rolls: Over or Under
text:
Over: 21 votes (84.0%) Under: 4 votes (16.0%) Margin: 17 votes (≈ 68.0% points)








Figure 1: Counts and shares.








Is this different from a 50/50 split? Yes. Exact binomial test p‑value = 9.11^{-4}.
“Over” share 95% CI: [0.64, 0.95].
How many voters would need to switch to change the winner? Because each switch changes the margin by 2, you’d need 9 people to switch from Over to Under to flip the result. A perfect tie isn’t possible with 25 voters.











Figure 2: Each square is one vote (25 total).

title: Toilet Paper Rolls: Over or Under
text:
Quick Look
Over: 21 votes (84.0%) Under: 4 votes (16.0%) Margin: 17 votes (≈ 68.0% points)








Figure 1: Counts and shares.

text:
